title: Partial Kondo Screening Solves the Mystery of Rare Earth Tetraborides
abstract: We invoke a new mechanism to account for multiple magnetization plateaus observed in rare-earth tetraborides. Using a combination of hybrid and semiclassical Monte Carlo simulations of the Kondo lattice model (KLM) on the Shastry-Sutherland lattice (SSL), we find robust magnetization plateaus at fractions 1/6, 2/9, 1/4, 1/3, 1/2, 2/3 and 3/4 of the saturation magnetization. We find that most of the plateau states are partially Kondo screened and emerge from the field-tuning of a complex three-way competition between the kinetic energy, the Kondo coupling, and the magnetic frustration. Most remarkably, the unusual magneto-transport reported in ErB$_4$ and TmB$_4$ admits an unexpectedly simple explanation within our mechanism. This work not only provides an elegant and simple solution to the long-standing puzzle of metamagnetism and anomalous magnetotransport in RB$_4$, but also introduces a novel mechanism to predict and discover new correlated phases in frustrated Kondo lattices.
summary: This paper proposes that partial Kondo screening, driven by the competition between kinetic energy, Kondo coupling, and magnetic frustration, is the key to understanding the complex magnetization plateaus in rare-earth tetraborides. The mechanism provides a unified explanation for both the variety of observed magnetic phases and the anomalous magnetotransport properties. This work introduces a novel way to predict new correlated phases in frustrated Kondo lattices.
main_problem: Explaining the mystery of multiple fractional magnetization plateaus and anomalous magnetotransport observed in rare-earth tetraborides (RB4).
main_result: The authors identify a 'partial Kondo screening' mechanism where a three-way competition between kinetic energy, Kondo coupling, and magnetic frustration stabilizes various magnetization plateaus and explains anomalous transport in ErB4 and TmB4.
method: The study uses a combination of Hybrid Monte Carlo (HMC) and Semiclassical Monte Carlo (SMC) simulations of the Kondo Lattice Model, supplemented by a variational ansatz to determine ground-state phase diagrams.
model_or_system: The system is the Kondo Lattice Model (KLM) on a Shastry-Sutherland lattice (SSL), representing metallic rare-earth tetraborides with localized moments and itinerant electrons.
key_observables: Normalized magnetization (m), fraction of Kondo singlets (nK), and electronic transport properties (longitudinal and Hall conductivities).
important_parameters: Kondo coupling (JK), electron hopping amplitudes (t1, t2), magnetic exchange interactions (J1, J2), and external magnetic field (hz).
main_assumptions: The use of a semiclassical approximation to represent local Kondo singlet formation and the treatment of electronic degrees of freedom via an effective one-particle quantum problem.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the KLM schematic on the SSL; Figure 3 illustrates real-space configurations of Up, Down, and Singlet sites and the phase diagram; Figure 4 displays magnetization and Kondo-singlet fraction versus magnetic field; Figure 5 shows real-space patterns for specific plateau states.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of RB4 magnetism, presents the KLM on the SSL model, details the Monte Carlo and variational methods, analyzes the emergence of magnetization plateaus through partial Kondo screening, and concludes by applying the mechanism to explain magnetotransport anomalies.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. A schematic representation of our semiclassical ver- sion of the KLM. Top layer is the tight-binding model on the SSL with t1, t2 denoting the hopping parameters on SSL. The bottom layer represents localized moments interacting via J1 and J2. The vertical ovals indicate local singlets at C sites. The U sites retain a Zeeman-like coupling of strength JK.

Figure 2. Variations of the normalized magnetization, m, and the fraction of Kondo singlets, nK, with external field, hz, for (a) JK = 0.0, (b) JK = 0.3, (c) JK = 0.6, and JK = 0.9. The symbols are the SMC data and the continuous lines are obtained from variational calculations (see Supplemental Material for details).
